APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper sits squarely in the NLP/ML venue scope: it proposes a variant of multi-document summarization (summary as concept maps), introduces a large annotated corpus (30 topics, large heterogeneous web clusters), and a novel crowdsourcing scheme (low-context importance annotation) — all topics of clear interest to the NLP community. The formulation (concept-map summarization), use of established resources and tools (DIP corpus, TAC2008, Open IE, TrueSkill), baseline system and standard evaluation metrics (strict matching, METEOR, ROUGE) match the methods and evaluation expectations of top NLP conferences. Related work is positioned against DUC/TAC and prior concept-map and graph-based summarization research, so the paper targets the right audience and is properly situated within the community.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important and under-resourced problem—summarizing very large, heterogeneous web document clusters as human-interpretable concept maps—and provides a concrete resource and protocol to enable work on it. It delivers a sizeable, publicly releasable corpus (30 topics, ~40 docs/topic, ~98k tokens/cluster), a novel low-context crowdsourcing scheme with demonstrated reliability (Likert/comparison correlations ~0.7–0.8) and cost footprint, plus baseline methods and evaluation metrics. By filling a clear dataset/evaluation gap and exposing concrete pipeline bottlenecks (content selection and relation extraction), the work is likely to change how researchers approach multi-document summarization and open several follow-up directions (concept-map generation, crowdsourced importance estimation, evaluation measures), making it of strong impact though not fully transformative.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents notable novelty by defining summarization as generation of summary concept maps and releasing a new large-scale corpus for heterogeneous, large document clusters (‘‘we present a newly created corpus of concept maps...’’; contrasts with Villalon et al. (2010) which is single-document). It also introduces a tailored crowdsourcing protocol, ‘‘low-context importance annotation’’, for obtaining importance estimates at scale and uses TrueSkill-based ranking to refine top propositions. At the same time, many techniques are composed from existing methods (Open IE for proposition extraction, TrueSkill for ranking, METEOR/ROUGE for evaluation), so the contribution is a clear, useful task/corpus and annotation method rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic technique.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents substantial experimental work: a pilot crowdsourcing study with reliability/replication statistics (Pearson 0.81/0.82 etc.), a peer-evaluation comparing importance scores to ROUGE/Pyramid, creation of a 30-topic DIP-derived corpus with detailed extraction/filtering (OpenIE confidence tuned on 500 tuples, threshold 0.5) and baseline system results with diagnostics (coverage and top-25 concept statistics, relation-extraction failures). It lacks formal ablation/sensitivity analyses of the baseline pipeline components (no controlled removal of features or alternative heuristics), though some sensitivity checks exist for OpenIE filtering and repeated crowdsourcing. Related work is covered well, failure modes and bottlenecks are discussed, and the authors report releasing the corpus, baseline and evaluation scripts—supporting reproducibility—though some low-level implementation/hyperparameter details for the baseline are not fully specified in the text.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper provides fair internal comparisons for its crowdsourcing scheme (pilot study vs TAC2008 and discussion of Lloret et al., §3, and peer-evaluation correlating with ROUGE/Pyramid) and presents a baseline and error analysis (§6). However, it does not experimentally compare its baseline or corpus-driven systems against prior automated concept-map or graph-based summarization methods (e.g. Villalon 2012, Qasim et al., Fang et al.), only citing them qualitatively. Thus relevant competing approaches are discussed and differences analyzed, but important empirical baselines are missing.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and follows a logical flow (intro → method: crowdsourcing and Pilot Study §3.2 → corpus construction §4 → analysis §5 → baseline experiments §6 → conclusion). The main contributions (a new low-context importance annotation scheme and a released concept-map corpus) are stated early/clearly (final paragraphs and abstract-like summary). Notation is minimal and mostly consistent (metrics and TrueSkill usage are described), but a few explanations (e.g., the "five meta-workers" footnote) are slightly unclear. Presentation is harmed by formatting artifacts and extensive repetition/mangled text (many duplicate sentences such as "Generated summaries have been shown..." and "Introduced in 1972..." and missing/garbled figure/table references like Figure 1 and Table 1/2), which makes some sections harder to read.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by empirical evidence: the pilot/corpus-scale crowdsourcing shows high agreement and repeatability (Likert Pearson ~0.80–0.82, comparisons majority ~0.73 and repeat correlations ~0.69–0.82), and the OpenIE filtering was tuned on held-out samples preserving 98.7% of correct extractions. The baseline experiments and error analysis (76% coverage at extraction but only 17% in top-25, poor relation extraction) substantiate the claimed challenges. Minor issues remain: some supporting results are only referenced (Table 1 not shown), details of statistical testing are absent, and the expert revision/annotation reliability is only briefly stated, but these do not undermine the main technical conclusions.
